---
title: "Paper Example"
author:
  - name: Victor van Pelt
    email: Victor.vanPelt@whu.edu
    affiliation: WHU Otto Beisheim School of Management
date: last-modified
date-format: long
execute:
  echo: false
  message: false
  warning: false
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    papersize: letter
    fig-pos: H
    fig_caption: yes
    fig-cap-location: top
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    ident: yes

editor: visual

always_allow_html: yes

bibliography: references.bib

header-includes:
  - \usepackage{setspace}\doublespacing
  - \setlength{\parindent}{4em}
  - \setlength{\parskip}{0em}
  - \setlength{\skip\footins}{2em}
  - \usepackage[hang]{footmisc}
  - \setlength{\footnotemargin}{1em}
  - \usepackage{pdfpages}
  - \usepackage{pdflscape}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{csquotes}
  - \renewcommand{\mkbegdispquote}[2]{\itshape}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# This chunk reads a number computed by the pipeline so the text updates
# automatically when the data change. It reads the *processed* data from
# 2_process, so run the pipeline first (e.g. `make all` or `make r`).
# It accepts either the R (.rds) or the Stata (.dta) output.
proc_rds <- "../2_process/edit_gen_ai_earnings.rds"
proc_dta <- "../2_process/edit_gen_ai_earnings.dta"

if (file.exists(proc_rds)) {
  data <- readRDS(proc_rds)
} else if (file.exists(proc_dta)) {
  if (!requireNamespace("haven", quietly = TRUE))
    install.packages("haven", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
  data <- haven::read_dta(proc_dta)
} else {
  stop("No processed data found. Run the pipeline first (e.g. `make all`).")
}

# Dynamic figure reused in the text below
sample_size <- nrow(data)
```


<!-- Title + Abstract Page -->
```{=tex}
\thispagestyle{empty}
\begin{abstract}
\singlespace
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur. Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident, sunt in culpa qui officia deserunt mollit anim id est laborum.
\end{abstract}
```
```{=tex}
\vspace{0.5cm}
\begin{center}
\textbf{Preliminary and Incomplete}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.75cm}
\begin{flushleft}
\textbf{Keywords}:
\newline
\textbf{Data Availability}:  \newline
\textbf{Declaration of Interest}: The author(s) declare(s) that they have no conflict of interest \newline
\end{flushleft}
```

\pagebreak

\setcounter{page}{1}

<!-- Main Document -->
## Introduction

This template shows how a paper can pull numbers and tables straight from the analysis pipeline, so the manuscript never falls out of sync with the data. For example, our estimation sample contains `r sample_size` observations, and that number is read (dynamically, from the processed data in `2_process`) rather than typed by hand.[^1] Citations work as usual: @HLL_2000 discuss reproducibility in accounting research, and the same point can be made parenthetically [@HLL_2000].

[^1]: You can also write in footnotes.

\newpage

## Results

We find a positive and significant association between generative AI use and scaled earnings. See Table 1 below.

\centerline{--- Insert Table 1 about here ---}

Because the table is generated by the pipeline and imported here, re-running the analysis on new data updates it automatically.

\newpage

## References {.unnumbered}

\singlespacing

\small

::: {#refs}
:::

\newpage

### Table 1: Example Table {.unnumbered}

```{=tex}
\begin{singlespace}
\setlength{\parindent}{0em}
\centering
\input{../3_output/table_1.tex}
\end{singlespace}
```
